content:
6 min read
2 hours ago

--

Over a million lines, 6,755 commits, merged in about a week. The runtime under Claude Code, rewritten by Claude. I keep re-reading that sentence and it keeps not getting easier.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

So I upgraded Bun on Friday morning, like I do most Fridays, bun upgrade, didn't think twice. Then I actually read the change log and just kind of sat there with my coffee going wait, what.
Here’s the short version, and I promise I’m not embellishing any of it.
Bun, the JavaScript runtime and toolkit, was written in Zig. Anthropic bought the company behind it, Oven, back in December. Claude Code itself ships as a Bun binary, so if you’ve ever run Claude Code, you’ve run Bun, probably without ever knowing.
And a couple weeks ago the Bun team pointed Claude at their own codebase and had it translate more or less the entire thing from Zig to Rust. The pull request, #30412, landed on May 14.
Six thousand seven hundred and fifty-five commits. North of a million lines added. The day before, they’d shipped v1.3.14, the last Zig release Bun will ever have.
An AI rewrote the runtime. The runtime that the AI runs on.

Okay, but why would you do this
This is the part that actually made me laugh, because the real trigger isn’t performance, and it isn’t even safety. It’s a policy.
Zig, the language, has a rule: no AI-generated code in the project. The Zig Software Foundation doesn’t want it. And the Bun team, who quite literally build an AI coding tool for a living, work in a deeply AI-heavy way. So their contributions kept bouncing off that wall.
They’d ended up running on a fork of Zig carrying changes that could never go upstream, which is its own special kind of misery if you’ve ever maintained a fork of a language toolchain.
There are real engineering reasons stacked on top, to be fair. Memory bugs in the Zig codebase had cost the team years of cumulative debugging, the segfault-at-2am stuff that just quietly bleeds hours.
Rust’s borrow checker takes a whole category of that off the table. Anthropic standardizes on Rust internally. And Claude, like every model, is plain better at Rust than at Zig, because there’s a mountain more Rust in the training data.
But strip it down and the headline is almost too clean.
The AI company couldn’t get its AI-written code into Zig, so it had its AI rewrite everything in a language that wouldn’t object. The snake, the tail, you know how it goes.

The genuinely impressive part
I want to be fair here, because my first instinct was to be snide and that’s the lazy read.
A port like this is normally where projects go to die. Rewriting a mature codebase from one systems language to another is the classic multi-year death march, the thing every greybeard warns you never to do, the call that sank real companies in the 2000s.
Bun did it in something like a week of wall-clock time. The existing test suite passes across Linux, macOS, and Windows. Binary size actually dropped a few megabytes. Benchmarks came out somewhere between no change and a touch faster.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

And the reason it worked is honestly a lesson worth keeping. Jarred Sumner, who created Bun, described the approach as holding the same architecture, the same data structures, almost no third-party crates, and pointedly no async Rust.
They didn’t let the model redesign a thing. The instruction was, roughly, behave exactly like the Zig version, just in Rust. The Zig code was the spec. The test suite was the judge. The model was the very fast, very tireless intern doing the mechanical translation in the middle.
That’s the thing AI is quietly, unreasonably good at, and it’s not what the demos sell you. It’s not build me a startup.
It’s here is a precise reference implementation and a test oracle, now translate it and do not get creative.” Constrained and verifiable and, honestly, a bit boring. That’s the sweet spot.

And yet
Here’s where my coffee went cold.
A few days before the merge, Sumner himself was telling people to calm down. His words, roughly: this whole thread is an overreaction, three hundred comments about code that doesn’t even work, we haven’t committed to rewriting, there’s a very high chance all of this gets thrown out. And then, days later, it merged into main.
I’m not dunking on him, plans move fast and that’s allowed. But the gap between this might all get deleted and this is now the only version was, what, a long weekend?
Then there’s the safety thing, which is the bit I can’t quite shake. One of the stated reasons for leaving Zig was memory safety. Rust, the safe language. Except people started reading the generated Rust, and it’s shot through with unsafe blocks.
Thousands of them, by some counts. And sure, a runtime needs some unsafe, that's normal this far down the stack. But you can't really wave the safety flag and then ship a wall of unsafe that a human wrote approximately none of. The safety you think you bought might be mostly a green test suite and good vibes.
Because that’s the other thing nobody can pretend around. No human read that diff. You cannot line-by-line review a million-line change, it is not physically possible.
So we reviewed it quietly becomes the tests passed and it felt right, and those tests were written for the Zig version, catching the behaviors somebody already thought to check. The bug nobody wrote a test for is now sitting somewhere inside six thousand commits that no person read end to end.
And it’s underneath me. That’s what keeps poking at me. This isn’t some repo I’ll never touch. Bun’s in my stack. Claude Code is open in the other window right now, running on a Bun binary that, as of two weeks ago, an AI wrote. The floor got swapped out while I was standing on it, and the contractor was the same tool I use to build the house.

What I actually think, for now
I keep landing in the same annoyingly un-spicy place: this was probably the right call, it still unsettles me, and both of those are true at the same time.
Right, because the constraints were perfect for it. A reference implementation, a hard test oracle, a human holding the architecture still, a translation job instead of a creative one. If you’re going to bet a million lines on a model, that is the only setup I’d do it under. I might even borrow the playbook for an ancient service at work I’ve been too scared to touch.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Unsettled, because the speed hides how much trust you’re handing over, and to what. We’ve spent two years arguing about whether to let AI write a single function.
This skipped straight to letting it rewrite the engine, in a week, in a repo millions of people lean on, with the original author publicly unsure of the plan days earlier.
Anyway. I ran bun --version after the upgrade. It printed a number.
Everything worked. My tests went green. And I genuinely can't tell you whether that's the reassuring part or the unsettling one, and I'm starting to think the fact that I can't tell is the whole story.
